encyclopedia.description: The Sun, also referred to as 'Sol', is the star at the center of the Solar System. Sun's mass accounts for some 99.86% of the total mass of the Solar System.

structureDescriptions.PHOTOSPHERE: The photosphere is a thin outermost layer of the Sun. Below photosphere, the Sun becomes opaque. However, the photosphere itself is only slightly less opaque than the air on Earth.
structureDescriptions.CONVECTIVE ZONE: Thermal columns carry hot material to the surface of the Sun. Once the material cools off, it plunges back towards the hot radiative zone.
structureDescriptions.RADIATIVE ZONE: Thermal radiation transfers the heat from inner core outward. Material in this zone is so dense that photons may take millions of years to travel through it.
structureDescriptions.CORE: Nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium creates the massive energy output of the Sun. Temperatures reach 15 million Kelvin.

encyclopedia.description: Mercury is the smallest and incredibly hot planet in the Solar System. It is the closest to the Sun and lacks any substantial atmosphere to retain its heat.

structureDescriptions.CRUST: Mercury's crust is composed of solid silicate rocks and is heavily cratered due to ancient impacts.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: The silicate mantle is relatively thin compared to Earth's, possibly heavily stripped by a massive ancient impact.
structureDescriptions.CORE: Mercury features a massive metallic core, comprising about 85% of its entire volume. It's partially molten relative to its size.

encyclopedia.description: Venus is the second planet from the Sun and is Earth's 'sister planet' due to their similar size. Its incredibly thick atmosphere traps heat, making it the hottest planet.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: Venus has a crushing atmosphere of 96.5% Carbon Dioxide with thick clouds of sulfuric acid, creating a runaway greenhouse effect.
structureDescriptions.CRUST: The surface is mostly volcanic with extensive plains and highlands composed of basaltic rock.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: A rocky mantle over 3000km thick, transferring heat via convection.
structureDescriptions.CORE: Venus likely has a partially liquid iron core, similar to Earth, though it lacks an internal magnetic dynamo.

encyclopedia.description: Earth is the third planet from the Sun and the only astronomical object known to harbor life. It has a complex and dynamic surface and atmosphere.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: A protective layer composed of 78% Nitrogen and 21% Oxygen, crucial for life and regulating temperature.
structureDescriptions.CRUST: Broken into tectonic plates that constantly shift over millions of years.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: Highly viscous, plastic rock that drives tectonic plate motion.
structureDescriptions.OUTER CORE: Molten iron and nickel flow creating Earth's defensive magnetosphere.
structureDescriptions.INNER CORE: Crystalized solid iron under immense pressure reaching temperatures as hot as the sun's surface.

encyclopedia.description: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun, a dusty, cold, desert world with a very thin atmosphere. It's often called the 'Red Planet' due to iron oxide rust.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: An extremely thin atmosphere mostly of Carbon Dioxide. Too thin to support liquid water on the surface today.
structureDescriptions.CRUST: Covered in iron oxide dust, featuring massive inactive volcanos and deep rift valleys.
structureDescriptions.CORE: A dense core estimated to be partially liquid, comprising mostly iron and a significant amount of lighter sulfur.

encyclopedia.description: Jupiter is the largest planet in the Solar System. It's a gas giant with a mass more than two and a half times that of all the other planets combined.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: Bandings of swirling clouds containing ammonia, water, and complex chemicals generating massive storms like the Great Red Spot.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: Under extreme pressure, hydrogen turns into a liquid, metallic fluid capable of conducting electricity, driving Jupiter's massive magnetic field.
structureDescriptions.CORE: The exact nature is unknown, but likely a dense central core of rock, metal, and hydrogen compounds spanning heavier elements.

encyclopedia.description: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and known for its dazzling, complex system of icy rings. It has the lowest density of any planet.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: Similar to Jupiter, made predominantly of Hydrogen and Helium. It features massive hexagonal storm patterns near the poles.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: A deep layer of liquid metallic hydrogen that functions similarly to Jupiter's inner layers.
structureDescriptions.CORE: An extremely hot, dense core surrounded by an envelope of lighter element ices and molecular hydrogen.

encyclopedia.description: Uranus is the seventh planet from the Sun. It rotates at a nearly 90-degree angle from the plane of its orbit, making it appear to spin on its side.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: The methane gas within the upper atmosphere absorbs red light, giving Uranus its pale cyan color.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: A hot, dense fluid of 'icy' materials—water, ammonia, and methane—swirling beneath the atmosphere.
structureDescriptions.CORE: A relatively small rocky core comprising silicate, iron, and nickel alloys.

encyclopedia.description: Neptune is the eighth planet, an ice giant defined by intense winds and a dark, cold, whip-lashing atmosphere. It was the first planet located through mathematical calculations.

structureDescriptions.ATMOSPHERE: Extremely active weather systems, including supersonic winds and massive dark storm configurations.
structureDescriptions.MANTLE: A superheated liquid region colloquially termed an 'icy' mantle. Conditions are so extreme that 'diamond rain' forms, sinking towards the core.
structureDescriptions.CORE: A solid core slightly larger than Earth's composed primarily of silicates and heavy metals.
